title: find_antediluvian_parallel data payload
description:
Structured payload for the `find_antediluvian_parallel` tool — takes a passage from a Jewish/Christian antediluvian-wisdom text (1 Enoch / Jubilees / Genesis 5-6) and returns ranked Mesopotamian source-candidates that the comparative-religion scholarship has identified as parallels.

Direction of comparison is Hebrew/Aramaic ← Mesopotamian. The tool surfaces both the proposed parallel(s) AND the scholar(s) who established each link, with their specific publications. This is comparative-religion as auditable evidence: a parallel without a scholar's name behind it is not returned.

Provenance is `source: local`, `endpoint: local:antediluvianParallelIndex`. Underlying alignment is curated from Lambert 1967, Kvanvig 1988, Annus 2010, Reed 2005, plus the Research/The_Watchers.md brief.

query.description: Echo of the input query, normalized.

text_id.description: Source text. `1_enoch` includes the Book of the Watchers (chs 1-36), Book of Parables (37-71), Astronomical Book (72-82), Book of Dreams (83-90), Epistle of Enoch (91-108).

topic.description: Optional topic-keyword if the caller did not supply a specific passage. E.g. `seventh_patriarch_ascent`, `fallen_angels_teaching_arts`, `nephilim_origin`. Returns the most-cited passage for that topic.

passage_text.description: The text of the queried passage, ~300-600 chars, in standard English translation. Translator named under `passage_translator`.

passage_translator.description: Translator for the passage_text. E.g. `Nickelsburg 2004` for 1 Enoch, `NRSV` for Genesis.

results.description: Ranked candidate parallels, strongest first. Ranking is by `correspondence_strength`. Each result names the scholar(s) who established the parallel; results without scholarly attribution are not returned.

text.description: Standard text name. E.g. `Bīt Mēseri`, `Sumerian King List (WB 444)`, `Atra-ḫasīs Tablet III`, `Erra and Išum`, `Adapa myth`, `Enmeduranki text (Lambert 1967)`.

citation.description: Line-range or tablet-section reference. E.g. `Bīt Mēseri III.lines 1-21`, `SKL WB 444 col.i.1-39`.

language.description: Original language of the cited text.

approximate_date.description: Approximate composition date of the Mesopotamian source. Free-form. E.g. `c. 2000 BCE`, `Neo-Assyrian (c. 700 BCE)`, `Old Babylonian recension c. 1700 BCE`.

parallel_type.description: Kind of parallel. `structural` = shared narrative architecture (e.g. seventh figure ascends); `lexical` = shared specific vocabulary or name; `narrative` = shared specific scene or event; `topos` = shared general motif (e.g. fish-cloaked sage); `onomastic` = name-correspondence (e.g. Enmeduranki ~ Enoch).

correspondence_strength.description: Scholarly consensus on the parallel. `strong` = widely accepted as direct influence or shared substrate (e.g. Enmeduranki-Enoch); `moderate` = generally accepted but with caveats; `weak` = proposed but with significant counter-arguments; `contested` = active scholarly disagreement.

scholarly_attribution.description: One or more publications that established or strongly developed this parallel. Required — no scholar, no result.

publication.description: Full citation: book or journal article. E.g. `Journal of Cuneiform Studies 21 (1967): 126-138`.

argument_summary.description: One-sentence summary of what this publication argued. E.g. `Established the seventh-antediluvian-king / seventh-patriarch structural parallel via shared celestial-knowledge motif`.

transmission_hypothesis.description: How the parallel is hypothesized to have reached the Jewish text. Optional. Most current scholarship favors a layered model (multiple of these in combination).

notes.description: Free-form scholarly note on the parallel — caveats, refinements, recent challenges. Up to ~300 chars.
